title: Indigenous Grief Tending and the Anger Inside
dek: When the sorrow of generations lodges in your jaw and psoas, the fury that follows is not a malfunction. It is the body's ancient, intelligent demand for a witness to the profound losses we were told to quietly endure.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Weight in the Jaw

You wake up at 3:14 AM, and the first thing you notice is the ache in your masseter muscles. Your jaw is clamped shut, grinding against itself as if trying to pulverize a stone that has been lodged in your mouth for centuries. This is not just stress from your inbox. This is the ancient, intelligent architecture of your body holding onto something that was never fully metabolized. The anger that wakes you in the dark is not a moral failure; it is a biological imperative. It is the sound of grief that has been denied its proper mourning.

When we talk about anger, we are so often talking about grief that has been forced into hiding. In the somatic landscape, grief and anger are not separate continents; they are the same river, just flowing at different speeds. The sorrow of generations, the profound losses of land, language, and lineage—these do not simply evaporate because we have learned to wear professional clothing and speak in measured tones. They settle into the fascia. They tighten the psoas. They restrict the diaphragm until every breath feels like a negotiation with a ghost.

Resmaa Menakem reminds us that trauma is not just an event that happened in the past; it is a physical response that lives in the body, passed down through the generations. When you feel that sudden, inexplicable surge of heat rising from your belly to your throat, you are not just reacting to the present moment. You are experiencing the reverberations of a history that your body remembers, even if your mind has forgotten. The anger is the body's way of saying, *This happened. It mattered. And it is not okay.*

## The Architecture of Sorrow

Consider the way your shoulders creep up toward your ears when you are trying to hold it all together. The levator scapulae and the upper trapezius become rigid, forming a protective armor around your vulnerable neck. This is the posture of someone who is bracing for impact, someone who has learned that the world is not a safe place to soften. But what happens when you finally allow yourself to feel the weight of what you are carrying?

In many Indigenous traditions, grief is not a private, shameful experience to be hidden away in a therapist's office. It is a communal event, a necessary tending to the tears that keep the world in balance. When we lose this communal container for grief, the sorrow turns inward. It ferments. It becomes the sharp, biting anger that snaps at the people we love most. It becomes the chronic inflammation that modern medicine tries to suppress with pills, ignoring the root cause of the fire.

Gabor Maté speaks eloquently about the connection between repressed emotion and physical illness. When we push down our anger, when we swallow our grief, the body absorbs the impact. The immune system becomes confused, attacking the self because it cannot identify the true enemy. The anger inside is not a pathology; it is a desperate attempt to protect the organism from further harm. It is the body's fierce, uncompromising demand for truth.

## The Medicine of the Earth

When the heat of anger threatens to consume you, the instinct is often to numb it, to distract yourself, to run away. But the body knows better. The body knows that the only way out is through. This is where the medicine of the earth comes in. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is associated with the Liver meridian, the organ responsible for the smooth flow of Qi and blood. When the Liver is stagnant, the anger becomes explosive, unpredictable.

You can support the Liver with bitter herbs—dandelion root, burdock, yellow dock. These are not just quaint remedies; they are profound allies in the work of emotional digestion. They stimulate the production of bile, helping the body to break down the heavy, rich foods of our modern diet, just as we must break down the heavy, rich emotions of our inherited trauma. A strong infusion of nettle leaf, steeped overnight, provides the deep mineral nourishment the nervous system needs to feel safe enough to release its grip.

[FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a simple, accessible way to begin this process. As you sip the bitter brew, notice how it lands in your stomach. Notice the slight astringency on your tongue. This is the taste of boundaries, the taste of a body learning to say *no* to what it can no longer digest.

## The Practice of Witnessing

How do we begin to tend to this ancient grief? How do we honor the anger without letting it destroy us? It begins with witnessing. It begins with dropping the story and turning toward the sensation.

Bessel van der Kolk has shown us that the body keeps the score, that the trauma is etched into our very physiology. But the body also holds the map to healing. When you feel the anger rising, do not try to talk yourself out of it. Do not tell yourself that you are being unreasonable, that you should be over it by now. Instead, locate the sensation in your body. Is it a tightness in your chest? A burning in your throat? A trembling in your hands?

Breathe into that space. Not to fix it, not to make it go away, but simply to let it know that it is seen. *I feel you,* you might say to the tightness in your chest. *I know you are there. I know you are carrying something heavy.* This is the essence of somatic practice: the radical act of befriending your own biology.

## The Communal Container

We were never meant to do this work alone. The idea of the solitary, self-sufficient individual is a modern myth, a dangerous illusion that cuts us off from the very resources we need to heal. In Indigenous cultures around the world, healing is a collective endeavor. When one person is sick, the whole community gathers to sing, to pray, to witness.

When we try to process our grief and anger in isolation, we are asking our nervous systems to do the impossible. We need the resonance of other bodies to help us regulate our own. We need the sound of other voices to remind us that we are not crazy, that our anger is a sane response to an insane world.

This is why finding your people is not a luxury; it is a survival strategy. Whether it is a circle of women sitting around a fire, a group of friends sharing a meal, or a community organizing for justice, we need spaces where our full, messy, furious humanity is welcome. We need spaces where we can wail, where we can shake, where we can let the grief move through us like a storm.

## The Alchemy of Fury

Anger, when it is honored and channeled, is a profound force for change. It is the energy that says *enough*. It is the fire that burns away the dead wood, making space for new growth. But this alchemy can only happen when the anger is rooted in love, when it is connected to the deep, abiding sorrow of what has been lost.

Thich Nhat Hanh taught that we must hold our anger like a crying baby. We do not yell at the baby; we do not throw the baby out. We cradle it. We soothe it. We ask it what it needs. When we hold our anger with this kind of tenderness, it begins to transform. The sharp, jagged edges soften. The heat becomes a steady, warming glow.

This is the work of the somatic guide, the herbalist, the human being. It is the work of turning toward the pain, of listening to the body's ancient wisdom, of tending to the grief until it becomes the soil from which our liberation can grow.

## Embodied Practice: The Jaw Release

Find a quiet place where you will not be disturbed. Sit or lie down in a comfortable position. Close your eyes and bring your attention to your jaw. Notice the tension there. Do not try to change it; just observe it.

Place the pads of your fingers gently on your masseter muscles, just in front of your earlobes. Apply a very light pressure, just enough to make contact with the skin. Imagine that your fingers are melting into the muscle, softening the tight, rigid fibers.

Take a slow, deep breath in through your nose, allowing your belly to expand. As you exhale, let out a soft sigh, allowing your jaw to drop open slightly. Repeat this three times.

Now, slowly and gently, begin to move your jaw from side to side. Notice any clicking, any resistance. Do not force it; just explore the range of motion.

Finally, open your mouth as wide as you comfortably can, as if you are about to let out a massive yawn. Hold it for a moment, feeling the stretch in the muscles, and then slowly close it.

Rest your hands in your lap and notice how your jaw feels now. Notice any changes in your breathing, in the tension in your neck and shoulders. This is the beginning of unraveling the knot.

## The Long Arc of Healing

Healing is not a destination; it is a continuous unfolding. It is the daily practice of returning to the body, of listening to its whispers before they become screams. It is the willingness to sit with the discomfort, to stay with the trouble, as Donna Haraway might say.

When we commit to this path, we are not just healing ourselves; we are healing our ancestors, and we are healing the generations to come. We are breaking the cycle of trauma and silence. We are reclaiming our right to feel, our right to be whole, our right to be fiercely, unapologetically alive.

The anger inside is not your enemy. It is your most loyal protector, your most honest messenger. It is the voice of the earth, crying out for justice, for balance, for a return to the sacred. Listen to it. Honor it. Let it guide you home.

## The Anatomy of Betrayal

When you have been taught that your anger is ugly, you learn to swallow it. You learn to smile when you want to scream. You learn to apologize for taking up space. But the body does not forget the betrayal. The body keeps a meticulous record of every time you abandoned yourself to keep the peace.

This betrayal lodges in the gut. It disrupts the microbiome, the complex ecosystem of bacteria that regulates your mood, your immunity, your very sense of self. The gut is the second brain, the seat of intuition. When you ignore your gut feelings, when you override your instinctual responses, you are severing the connection to your deepest wisdom.

To heal this betrayal, you must begin to rebuild trust with your own body. You must learn to listen to the subtle signals—the flutter in your stomach, the tightening in your chest, the sudden drop in your energy. These are not random fluctuations; they are precise, intelligent communications from a body that is trying to keep you safe.

## The Ritual of Return

How do we return to the body after so much time away? It requires patience. It requires a willingness to be clumsy, to make mistakes, to feel foolish. It requires a commitment to practice, not perfection.

Start small. Notice the feeling of your feet on the ground. Notice the temperature of the air on your skin. Notice the rhythm of your breath. These simple acts of attention are the building blocks of somatic awareness. They are the breadcrumbs that will lead you back to yourself.

As you build this capacity for presence, you will inevitably encounter the pain that you have been avoiding. The grief will rise up, thick and choking. The anger will flare, hot and terrifying. When this happens, do not run. Stay. Breathe. Remember that you are not alone. Remember that you are held by the earth, by the ancestors, by the vast, unbroken web of life.

## The Sovereignty of the Somatic

Ultimately, this work is about sovereignty. It is about reclaiming the authority of your own experience. It is about refusing to let anyone else dictate what is true for your body.

When you are grounded in your somatic reality, you become unshakeable. You can navigate the chaos of the world with grace and resilience. You can set boundaries without guilt. You can love without losing yourself.

This is the promise of the somatic path. It is not an easy journey, but it is the only one worth taking. It is the journey from fragmentation to wholeness, from silence to song, from fear to freedom.

## The Wisdom of the Weeds

Look to the plants for guidance. Look to the weeds that push their way through the cracks in the pavement, refusing to be eradicated. They are the ultimate survivors, the masters of adaptation.

[Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) is one such weed. It sends its taproot deep into the earth, drawing up minerals that have been leached from the topsoil. It is a stubborn, tenacious plant, and it offers that same tenacity to us. When we consume burdock, we are taking in the medicine of deep nourishment, of grounding, of unearthing what has been buried.

Similarly, [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) is the fierce protector of the heart. Its Latin name, *Leonurus cardiaca*, means "lion-hearted." It is the herb for the mother who is exhausted, overwhelmed, and pushed to the brink. It soothes the frayed nerves, calms the racing heart, and reminds us that we have the strength of a lioness within us.

## The Legacy of the Lineage

We are the culmination of thousands of generations of survivors. The blood that flows through our veins is the same blood that flowed through the veins of those who endured unimaginable hardship, who fought for their lives, who kept the sacred fires burning in the darkest of times.

When we heal our own trauma, we are honoring their legacy. We are ensuring that their suffering was not in vain. We are weaving a new story, a story of resilience, of joy, of profound connection to the earth and to each other.

The anger inside is the spark that ignites this transformation. It is the catalyst for the alchemy of healing. Do not fear it. Do not suppress it. Welcome it as the sacred messenger it is.

## The Final Exhalation

As you come to the end of this exploration, take a moment to acknowledge the courage it takes to turn toward the pain. It is not easy work, but it is the most important work you will ever do.

Let out a long, slow exhalation. Imagine that you are releasing all the tension, all the fear, all the doubt that you have been carrying. Let it go into the earth, where it can be composted and transformed into new life.

You are not broken. You are not flawed. You are a magnificent, complex, deeply feeling human being, and your body is a miracle of resilience and grace. Trust it. Listen to it. Let it lead you home.

## The Deep Tissue of Memory

When we speak of memory, we usually mean the cognitive recall of events, the stories we tell ourselves about what happened. But the body has its own memory, a deep tissue archive that records every impact, every shock, every moment of profound connection or devastating loss. This somatic memory does not speak in words; it speaks in sensation, in posture, in the sudden tightening of the gut or the unexpected release of tears.

To access this memory, we must learn the language of the body. We must become fluent in the subtle dialects of tension and relaxation, of heat and cold, of expansion and contraction. This requires a radical shift in attention, a turning away from the endless chatter of the mind and a turning toward the quiet, persistent voice of the flesh.

When you begin to listen to this voice, you may be surprised by what you hear. You may discover that the chronic pain in your lower back is not just a structural issue, but a holding pattern, a physical manifestation of the burden you have been carrying for your family, your ancestors, your culture. You may find that the shallowness of your breath is not just a habit, but a survival strategy, a way of minimizing your presence in a world that has told you that you are too much.

## The Courage to Feel

It takes immense courage to feel the full spectrum of human emotion. We live in a culture that prizes comfort, convenience, and control, a culture that pathologizes pain and pathologizes the intense, messy, unpredictable expressions of grief and anger. We are taught to medicate our sorrow, to manage our rage, to keep our emotional lives neatly contained within the boundaries of polite society.

But the body knows that this containment is a form of violence. The body knows that emotion is energy in motion, and that when this energy is blocked, it stagnates, it ferments, it becomes toxic. To heal, we must allow the energy to move. We must allow the grief to wash over us like a tidal wave, trusting that we will not drown. We must allow the anger to burn through us like a wildfire, trusting that it will clear the brush and make way for new growth.

This is the work of the somatic warrior. It is the work of standing in the fire of your own experience, refusing to flinch, refusing to turn away. It is the work of claiming your right to feel, your right to be fully, unapologetically alive.

## The Sanctuary of the Senses

In the midst of the storm, the senses are your sanctuary. They are the anchors that keep you tethered to the present moment, the lifelines that pull you back from the abyss of dissociation and despair. When the grief threatens to overwhelm you, when the anger threatens to consume you, turn to your senses.

Notice the play of light and shadow on the wall. Listen to the sound of the wind in the trees, the distant hum of traffic, the rhythm of your own heartbeat. Feel the texture of the fabric against your skin, the weight of your body sinking into the chair, the solid, unyielding support of the earth beneath your feet.

Smell the damp earth after a rain, the sharp, clean scent of pine needles, the rich, complex aroma of a brewing cup of tea. Taste the bitterness of the dandelion root, the sweetness of the honey, the salt of your own tears.

These sensory experiences are not just distractions; they are profound acts of regulation. They signal to the nervous system that you are safe, that you are here, that you are alive. They are the antidotes to the numbing, the freezing, the checking out that trauma so often induces.

## The Alchemy of Presence

Ultimately, the healing of our deepest wounds is not a matter of fixing what is broken, but of bringing presence to what has been abandoned. It is the alchemy of turning toward the pain with a fierce, unwavering compassion, of holding the shattered pieces of ourselves in the light of our own awareness.

When we do this, something miraculous happens. The pain does not necessarily disappear, but it changes. It loses its sharp, jagged edges. It becomes integrated into the larger tapestry of our lives, a thread of dark, rich color that adds depth and texture to the whole.

This is the gift of the somatic path. It is the gift of wholeness, of integration, of coming home to the body, home to the earth, home to the sacred, beating heart of the world.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I start crying, I will never stop. Is this normal?**
**A:** Yes, it is profoundly normal. When you have been holding back a river of grief for decades, the dam breaking feels catastrophic. But the body has a natural rhythm of release and rest. You will not cry forever. The parasympathetic nervous system will eventually engage, bringing you back to baseline. The fear of the endless weeping is usually worse than the weeping itself. Trust the biological wave.

**Q: How do I know the difference between healthy anger and destructive rage?**
**A:** Healthy anger is clarifying. It moves through the body, delivers its message ("a boundary was crossed," "this is unjust"), and then it dissipates, leaving you more grounded and present. Destructive rage is a loop. It is often a secondary emotion covering up deep vulnerability or terror. It feels chaotic, disembodying, and leaves you exhausted and disconnected. Somatic practice helps you slow down enough to feel the difference in your tissues.

**Q: Can herbs really help with emotional trauma?**
**A:** Herbs are not a magic bullet that will erase your trauma, but they are powerful allies in regulating the nervous system. Plants like skullcap, milky oats, and motherwort work directly on the physiological manifestations of trauma—the hypervigilance, the racing heart, the frayed nerves. By supporting the physical body, they create a safer container for the emotional processing to occur. They remind the body what it feels like to be held and nourished.

**Q: I don't have access to a community for grief tending. What can I do?**
**A:** Start with the more-than-human world. The earth is the original container for our grief. Go to a body of water, a forest, or even a single tree in a city park. Speak your grief out loud to the land. The earth can hold the weight of your sorrow without collapsing. From there, seek out virtual spaces, somatic experiencing practitioners, or small local circles. The community is out there, but you must first signal to your nervous system that it is safe to connect.
